fact: Rome's insulae (apartment blocks) were stacked up to seven stories high and built largely of wood
source: Tacitus, Annals, Book XV, Chapters 38–44; also discussed in 'Ancient Rome: The Archaeology of the Eternal City' by Jon Coulston & Hazel Dodge (2000)

fact: The Great Fire of Rome began in shops near the Circus Maximus (described here as an oil shop) in July 64 AD
Rome's vigiles served as both night watchmen and firefighters, using buckets, blankets, and other rudimentary equipment
source: Rainbird, J.S., 'The Fire Stations of Imperial Rome,' Papers of the British School at Rome, Vol. 54 (1986)

fact: Rome's narrow streets — some only a few feet wide — created a chimney effect that accelerated the fire's spread
source: Tacitus, Annals, Book XV, Chapter 38; also discussed in 'Rome: An Urban History from Antiquity to the Present' by Rabun Taylor, Katherine Rinne, and Spiro Kostof (2016)

fact: Nero blamed and persecuted Christians for the fire, subjecting them to brutal executions
After the fire, Nero enacted building codes mandating wider streets, fire-resistant materials, and height limits for new construction
source: Tacitus, Annals, Book XV, Chapter 43; Suetonius, Lives of the Twelve Caesars, 'Nero,' Chapter 16
